stem: A 56-year-old man attends his GP for review of fatigue and nocturia over the last three months. He has hypertension on perindopril 5 mg, BMI 31, and his father has T2DM. He has had two haemoglobinopathy screens incidentally documented in the past (beta thalassaemia minor). He is not on glucocorticoids and has had no transfusions or recent illness. A fasting venous plasma glucose taken in a NATA-accredited laboratory two weeks ago was 7.3 mmol/L; today's laboratory HbA1c is 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent); a random venous glucose today is 9.4 mmol/L. He has acanthosis nigricans and is otherwise well, with no polyuria, weight loss, or vomiting.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/84 mmHg, HR 78 bpm, BMI 31 kg/m^2.
data_table.Examination: Acanthosis nigricans over the posterior neck. No ketotic breath. Hydrated.
data_table.Investigations: Fasting venous glucose 7.3 mmol/L (two weeks ago, NATA lab); HbA1c 50 mmol/mol (6.7 percent) today, standardised assay; random glucose 9.4 mmol/L today; eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2; ACR 1.2 mg/mmol.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step to establish or refute the diagnosis of type 2 diabetes?

options.text: Diagnose type 2 diabetes now on the basis of HbA1c 50 mmol/mol plus fasting glucose 7.3 mmol/L and commence metformin
options.rationale: Beta thalassaemia minor alters red cell turnover and can falsely lower or raise HbA1c depending on the assay; Australian Diabetes Society guidance lists haemoglobinopathy as a setting where HbA1c should not be used, requiring confirmation by repeat glycaemic testing (ADS Position; RACGP).

options.text: Repeat the fasting venous plasma glucose in a NATA-accredited laboratory on a separate day to confirm fasting glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L
options.rationale: Because his haemoglobinopathy invalidates HbA1c, Australian diagnosis must rely on two abnormal glycaemic tests on separate days (fasting glucose at least 7.0, OGTT 2-hour at least 11.1, or random at least 11.1 with classic symptoms); repeating the fasting glucose is the most direct confirmatory test (RACGP; ADS).

options.text: Discharge without diagnosis because the random glucose of 9.4 mmol/L is below 11.1 mmol/L
options.rationale: An isolated random glucose below 11.1 does not exclude diabetes; his fasting glucose of 7.3 mmol/L already meets the diabetes threshold and requires confirmatory testing, not discharge (RACGP).

options.text: Order fructosamine as the primary diagnostic test since HbA1c is unreliable in haemoglobinopathy
options.rationale: Fructosamine is not validated as a primary diagnostic test for diabetes in Australian guidelines; confirmation uses standard glycaemic tests (fasting glucose or OGTT) (ADS).

options.text: Order an oral glucose tolerance test with 2-hour glucose at 75 g load, but commence metformin today regardless of result
options.rationale: Initiating treatment before establishing diagnosis bypasses confirmation; OGTT is an acceptable confirmatory test but treatment must follow the diagnosis (RACGP; ADS).

explanation.summary: In an asymptomatic adult, Australian guidance diagnoses type 2 diabetes by two abnormal tests on separate occasions: fasting venous glucose at least 7.0 mmol/L, HbA1c at least 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on a standardised assay, OGTT 2-hour at least 11.1 mmol/L, or a single random glucose at least 11.1 mmol/L plus classic symptoms (RACGP 2024).
key_points: Fasting glucose >= 7.0 mmol/L is diagnostic when confirmed
HbA1c >= 48 mmol/mol (6.5 percent) on standardised assay is diagnostic when confirmed
Random glucose >= 11.1 mmol/L is diagnostic only with classic symptoms
Two abnormal results required in asymptomatic patients
explanation.pearls: Avoid HbA1c if haemoglobinopathy, recent blood loss, transfusion, pregnancy or rapidly changing glycaemia.

stem: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 9 years attends his GP for diabetes review. He is on metformin XR 2 g daily and has been adherent for 18 months. He has heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (LVEF 35 percent) on echocardiography after an anterior STEMI two years ago, treated with stented LAD, and is on perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg and aspirin 100 mg. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2 and ACR 6.4 mg/mmol on two occasions. He has no symptoms of urinary tract infection, no recurrent thrush and no history of diabetic foot ulceration. BMI is 29 kg/m^2 and he is normotensive. His most recent HbA1c is 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent), up from 56 mmol/mol six months ago. He denies hypoglycaemic episodes and is keen to optimise his cardiovascular and renal protection in addition to glycaemic control. He has no history of pancreatitis and no personal or family history of medullary thyroid carcinoma or MEN2. He has never had a diabetic foot ulcer, retinal laser or amputation. He is on no NSAID, no potassium-sparing diuretic and has no documented contrast allergy. He fully understands sick-day rules and is independently mobile, performs his own injections if needed and has reliable transport for monitoring bloods.

data_table.Examination: JVP not elevated. Chest clear. No peripheral oedema. Feet warm, pulses present, monofilament intact.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 62 mmol/mol (7.8 percent); eGFR 48 mL/min/1.73m^2; ACR 6.4 mg/mmol; potassium 4.2 mmol/L; LFTs normal; LVEF 35 percent.
data_table.Medications: Metformin XR 2 g, perindopril 8 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, atorvastatin 80 mg, aspirin 100 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?

options.text: Add a sulfonylurea such as gliclazide MR 30 mg daily
options.rationale: Sulfonylureas lower HbA1c but carry hypoglycaemia risk, do not improve cardiorenal outcomes, and offer no benefit in HFrEF or CKD progression. The anti-pattern is reaching for legacy HbA1c-only agents in a patient with cardiorenal indications. Gliclazide would be reasonable in T2DM without HFrEF/CKD when cost is constraining or as adjunct to glucose lowering (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Position Statement on T2DM).

options.text: Add an SGLT2 inhibitor such as empagliflozin 10 mg daily
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in HFrEF and slow CKD progression with albuminuria; they are first-choice add-on for T2DM with established HFrEF and CKD with eGFR within approved range (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; Heart Foundation HFrEF guideline 2022; KHA-CARI / KDIGO 2022).

options.text: Add basal insulin glargine starting 10 units nocte
options.rationale: Basal insulin lowers HbA1c but provides no cardiovascular outcome benefit and would be premature when oral and injectable non-insulin options remain. Insulin is correct when patient is glucotoxic (HbA1c >10%, ketonaemia, marked symptoms) or after exhausting oral/GLP-1 RA options (ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

options.text: Add a DPP4 inhibitor such as sitagliptin 100 mg daily
options.rationale: DPP4 inhibitors are weight-neutral, well tolerated, but cardiorenally neutral. Saxagliptin specifically increases HF hospitalisation (SAVOR-TIMI 53). The anti-pattern is choosing weight neutrality over outcome benefit. DPP4 inhibitors are reasonable as third-line in absence of HFrEF/CKD where SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA are contraindicated (ADS-ADEA Algorithm; AMH).

options.text: Add pioglitazone 15 mg daily for insulin sensitisation
options.rationale: Thiazolidinediones cause fluid retention via PPAR-gamma sodium reabsorption and are contraindicated in HFrEF (NYHA III/IV). Pioglitazone has glucose-lowering and modest cardiovascular benefits in selected patients, but never with established HFrEF (AMH; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

explanation.summary: In T2DM with established HFrEF, CKD with albuminuria, or atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, an SGLT2 inhibitor is the preferred add-on regardless of HbA1c, because cardiovascular death, heart failure hospitalisation and CKD progression are reduced independently of glucose lowering (ADS-ADEA; Heart Foundation 2022; KDIGO 2022).
key_points: SGLT2i indicated in HFrEF irrespective of diabetes status
SGLT2i slows CKD progression when ACR is elevated and eGFR is above the threshold (typically >=20 mL/min/1.73m^2 for empagliflozin / dapagliflozin)
Counsel on euglycaemic DKA, sick-day rules, genital mycotic infection
GLP1 receptor agonist (semaglutide, dulaglutide) is the preferred add-on if ASCVD predominates and weight loss is needed
explanation.pearls: Hold SGLT2 inhibitor during intercurrent illness, fasting, or pre-operatively to reduce euglycaemic DKA risk.

stem: A 71-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes for 14 years attends her GP. She is on metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg and dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly. She has tolerated triple therapy for 18 months without hypoglycaemia, weight loss has plateaued and her HbA1c has risen from 58 to 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent) over the last six months. She has no acute illness, drinks no alcohol and lives independently with her husband who can assist with injections. She is willing to start insulin but anxious about night-time hypoglycaemia. Her eGFR is 62 mL/min/1.73m^2, weight 78 kg, and she has retinopathy on annual review. Fasting capillary glucose self-monitoring shows readings of 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner readings are 8 to 10 mmol/L. There is no significant post-prandial glucose excursion documented. She drives a private car only and her vision is corrected to 6/9. She is up to date with her sick-day plan for the SGLT2 inhibitor.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, weight 78 kg.
data_table.Investigations: HbA1c 76 mmol/mol (9.1 percent); eGFR 62; fasting capillary glucose 11 to 14 mmol/L; pre-dinner 8 to 10 mmol/L.
data_table.Medications: Metformin XR 2 g, empagliflozin 25 mg, dulaglutide 1.5 mg weekly.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate insulin regimen to commence?

options.text: Premixed biphasic insulin (e.g. NovoMix 30) twice daily before breakfast and dinner
options.rationale: Premixed biphasic insulin covers basal and prandial but has the highest hypoglycaemia risk and least flexibility. This would be correct only when adherence to multi-injection regimens is poor and structured meal patterns are reliable; not first-line for isolated fasting hyperglycaemia (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Basal-bolus regimen with insulin glargine nocte and rapid-acting insulin with each meal
options.rationale: Basal-bolus is reserved for failure of basal alone or significant post-prandial excursions; this patient has isolated fasting hyperglycaemia. The anti-pattern is starting with maximal complexity when minimal intervention suffices. Basal-bolus is correct after basal optimisation if post-prandial glucose remains uncontrolled (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Long-acting basal insulin glargine 10 units nocte titrated by 2 units every 3 days
options.rationale: Once-daily long-acting basal insulin is the preferred initiation strategy in T2DM with elevated fasting glucose; start 10 units (or 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg) nocte and titrate to fasting target, continuing metformin, SGLT2i and GLP1 RA (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA Algorithm).

options.text: Twice-daily NPH insulin in equal split doses administered each morning and again each night
options.rationale: NPH has a 4 to 8 hour peak with higher nocturnal hypoglycaemia risk than long-acting analogues; long-acting analogues (glargine, detemir, degludec) are preferred especially in older patients fearful of hypoglycaemia. NPH may be acceptable on cost grounds or in patients without nocturnal hypoglycaemia concern (RACGP 2024).

options.text: Prandial-only rapid-acting insulin aspart given with only the largest carbohydrate meal of the day
options.rationale: Prandial-only insulin addresses post-prandial excursions, not fasting hyperglycaemia, and would miss her dominant abnormality. The anti-pattern is matching insulin profile to the wrong glycaemic pattern. Prandial-only would be correct with isolated post-prandial spikes and normal fasting glucose (RACGP 2024).

explanation.summary: Insulin initiation in type 2 diabetes is indicated when HbA1c is above target despite maximally tolerated oral and injectable non-insulin therapy, or in symptomatic hyperglycaemia, or perioperative or pregnancy contexts. The Australian standard is a once-daily long-acting basal analogue (glargine, degludec, or detemir) given at bedtime, starting at 10 units (or 0.1-0.2 units/kg), with patient-led self-titration of 2 units every 3 days targeting fasting capillary glucose 6-8 mmol/L. Existing metformin, SGLT2 inhibitor and GLP-1 RA continue - their cardiorenal and weight benefits are preserved. Sulfonylureas usually stop with insulin start to reduce hypoglycaemia risk. Counsel on hypoglycaemia recognition (sweating, tremor, hunger, confusion, irritability), 15-15-15 rule (15 g fast-acting carbohydrate, recheck in 15 minutes, repeat once), glucagon availability for severe hypoglycaemia, sick-day rules (continue insulin during illness, increase glucose monitoring), and Austroads driving guidance (check BGL before driving, do not drive under 5 mmol/L).
key_points: Start 10 units nocte or 0.1 to 0.2 units/kg/day
Titrate by 2 units every 3 days based on fasting capillary glucose
Continue oral agents and GLP1 RA
Add prandial insulin only if basal target is met but HbA1c is still high
explanation.pearls: When non-insulin therapy fails in type 2 diabetes, start once-daily long-acting basal insulin (glargine 10 units nocte, titrated by 2 units every 3 days to a fasting capillary glucose 6-8 mmol/L). Continue metformin, SGLT2i and GLP-1 RA (RACGP 2024; ADS-ADEA).

stem: An 18-year-old woman with newly diagnosed type 1 diabetes is admitted to a regional ED with severe DKA. Capillary glucose is 38 mmol/L, ketones 6.4 mmol/L, venous pH 6.98, HCO3 5 mmol/L, sodium 128 mmol/L, potassium 3.6 mmol/L, urea 11 mmol/L, creatinine 132 micromol/L. She received 2 L of 0.9 percent saline over the first hour for shock, followed by 0.9 percent saline at 500 mL/hour and a fixed-rate insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hour. Five hours into treatment, blood glucose has fallen to 12 mmol/L and bicarbonate has risen to 11 mmol/L, but she develops a worsening frontal headache, increasing drowsiness with a GCS drop from 15 to 11, a single episode of vomiting and a slowing heart rate. Her blood pressure has risen from 102/64 to 142/86 mmHg. Capillary glucose is now 11 mmol/L and ketones 2.4 mmol/L. The receiving consultant is concerned about a deteriorating neurological picture. A bedside neurological exam reveals sluggish pupils and brisk lower limb reflexes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/86 mmHg (up from 102/64), HR 58 bpm (down from 118), RR 18, SpO2 99 percent, T 36.6, GCS 11 (E3 V3 M5).
data_table.Examination: Drowsy. Sluggish pupillary response, no focal weakness. Brisk LL reflexes.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 11 mmol/L (was 38); ketones 2.4; pH 7.18, HCO3 11; Na 130, K 3.8.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?

options.text: Increase the IV fluid infusion rate to correct the remaining estimated deficit much faster
options.rationale: Excessive fluid administration is a major contributor to cerebral oedema in paediatric DKA; the appropriate response to suspected cerebral oedema is to HALVE or pause maintenance fluids, not increase them. This is one of the most dangerous anti-patterns in DKA management (BSPED/RCH CPG DKA).

options.text: Hypertonic saline 5 mL/kg over 10 minutes, halve fluids, head up, CT and retrieval
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline (or mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg) is first-line for suspected cerebral oedema in DKA. Halving fluids, head-up positioning, securing airway and urgent imaging plus retrieval to a tertiary centre are concurrent priorities (RCH CPG: DKA; BSPED 2020).

options.text: Stop the IV insulin infusion entirely and simply observe her for any spontaneous improvement
options.rationale: Stopping insulin allows ketogenesis to resume and does not treat rising ICP; insulin should be continued (often reduced) while neuroprotective measures are delivered. The anti-pattern is removing the etiologic treatment. Insulin can be reduced (e.g., to 0.05 units/kg/hr) but not stopped (RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Give IV 50 percent dextrose 50 mL because the cause of drowsiness is hypoglycaemia
options.rationale: Glucose 11 mmol/L is not hypoglycaemic; when DKA glucose falls below 14 mmol/L, the correct action is to ADD 5 or 10% dextrose to the maintenance fluid rather than push concentrated dextrose. The anti-pattern is treating any low-normal sugar as hypoglycaemia in a critically ill patient (RCH CPG: DKA).

options.text: Administer IV ceftriaxone 2 g and dexamethasone 10 mg for presumed bacterial meningitis
options.rationale: While headache and drowsiness raise meningitis as a differential, the pattern of Cushing reflex (hypertension, bradycardia) plus timing in DKA make cerebral oedema overwhelmingly likely. Empirical antibiotics should not delay osmotherapy; meningitis workup is correct only after stabilisation if features persist (RCH CPG: DKA).

explanation.summary: Cerebral oedema is a feared complication of DKA, especially in younger patients and the first 4 to 12 hours of treatment. Risk factors include severe acidosis, low partial pressure of CO2, raised urea, bicarbonate administration and aggressive fluid replacement. Treatment is osmotherapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol), reduced fluid rate, head-up positioning and urgent imaging plus retrieval (RCH CPG; BSPED 2020).
key_points: Triad of headache, decreased GCS and Cushing reflex in DKA = cerebral oedema until proven otherwise
Hypertonic saline 3 percent 5 mL/kg OR mannitol 0.5 to 1 g/kg
Halve maintenance fluids, head up 30 degrees, secure airway if GCS drops further
Do NOT correct sodium too rapidly; corrected sodium should rise as glucose falls
explanation.pearls: Late teens with first presentation DKA, severe acidosis and hyponatraemia carry the highest risk - low threshold for retrieval to a paediatric ICU.

stem: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes is brought from his nursing home with one week of progressive confusion and reduced oral intake. He has hypertension, chronic kidney disease and a recent UTI treated with trimethoprim. His regular medications include metformin 500 mg BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg and lisinopril 10 mg. Staff report he has been refusing to drink for the last two days. On arrival he is drowsy, severely dehydrated and has a temperature of 38.4 degrees. Capillary glucose is 48 mmol/L. Venous gas shows pH 7.34, bicarbonate 20 mmol/L, anion gap 14, lactate 1.8. Capillary ketones are 0.6 mmol/L. Sodium 152 mmol/L, potassium 4.1, urea 24, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 110), corrected sodium for glucose 158 mmol/L. Calculated effective serum osmolality is 348 mosm/kg. Urinalysis confirms leucocytes and nitrites; he is on no SGLT2 inhibitor. There are no Kussmaul respirations and no ketotic breath.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/56 mmHg, HR 112, RR 18, T 38.4, SpO2 96 percent on RA, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Severely dehydrated, dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 4 seconds. No Kussmaul breathing.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 48 mmol/L; ketones 0.6 mmol/L; pH 7.34, HCO3 20, AG 14; Na 152, K 4.1, Cr 198 (baseline 110); effective osmolality 348 mosm/kg.

lead_in: Which feature best distinguishes this presentation as hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS) rather than DKA?

options.text: The blood glucose level of 48 mmol/L on bedside capillary testing today
options.rationale: Severe hyperglycaemia (>30 mmol/L) occurs in both HHS and DKA; glucose level alone does not discriminate. The anti-pattern is using a number that is shared between the two diagnoses to differentiate. HHS typically has higher glucose (often >40) but overlap with severe DKA is substantial (eTG Endocrinology - HHS).

options.text: Effective osmolality above 320, capillary ketones under 3 and pH above 7.30 without acidosis
options.rationale: Diagnostic features of HHS are marked hyperglycaemia (typically >30 mmol/L), effective osmolality >320 mosm/kg, minimal ketosis (capillary ketones <3 mmol/L) and absence of significant acidosis (pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15) (JBDS HHS 2022; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: The acute kidney injury with creatinine rising from baseline 110 to 198 micromol/L
options.rationale: AKI is common in both DKA and HHS due to volume depletion and does not differentiate. This is the classic shared-feature anti-pattern. AKI severity may favour HHS due to more prolonged osmotic diuresis but is not discriminatory (KDIGO; eTG).

options.text: The presence of a urinary tract infection acting as the precipitating event here
options.rationale: Infection precipitates both HHS and DKA; the precipitant cannot discriminate the metabolic phenotype. The discrimination is biochemical (ketosis and acidosis present in DKA, absent or minimal in HHS), not aetiological (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: The reduced Glasgow Coma Scale score on arrival to the emergency department
options.rationale: Drowsiness can occur in both, although depressed consciousness correlates more closely with osmolality (and therefore is more often severe in HHS). However the DEFINING feature remains the biochemistry pattern (ketones, pH, osmolality), not GCS alone. GCS is supportive but not the specific discriminator (JBDS HHS 2022).

explanation.summary: HHS is defined biochemically by severe hyperglycaemia, effective osmolality >320 mosm/kg, minimal ketosis and absence of significant acidosis. Fluid resuscitation (typically 0.9 percent saline at a slower rate than DKA), VTE prophylaxis and identification of the precipitant are priorities; insulin is added at a lower rate (0.05 units/kg/hour) only after fluids restore perfusion (JBDS HHS 2022; eTG).
key_points: HHS biochemistry: glucose >30, osm >320, ketones <3, pH >=7.30, HCO3 >=15
Slower fluid replacement than DKA (older, comorbid, risk of fluid overload)
Mortality up to 20 percent - higher than DKA
Always give thromboprophylaxis (LMWH unless contraindicated)
explanation.pearls: If ketones are >3 mmol/L and there is acidosis, treat as mixed DKA/HHS using DKA pathway.

stem: A 74-year-old woman with T2DM on metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 120 mg daily and insulin glargine 22 units nocte is brought to ED by paramedics after her daughter found her diaphoretic and confused. Capillary glucose was 1.8 mmol/L pre-hospital. She received IM glucagon 1 mg followed by oral juice with rapid clinical improvement; her glucose now is 6.4 mmol/L and she is fully oriented. Over the last week she has reported anorexia, fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 2 kg. She started cefalexin five days ago for a urinary tract infection and the same day her GP added trimethoprim 300 mg daily after sensitivities returned. She has CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2 baseline). She lives alone and reports occasional missed meals. Her last HbA1c three months ago was 49 mmol/mol (6.6 percent). She has no chest pain. ECG shows sinus rhythm rate 78 with no acute ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/72 mmHg, HR 78, T 36.6, SpO2 98 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Now alert and oriented. No focal neurology. Mild dry mucous membranes.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose now 6.4 mmol/L; eGFR 38; HbA1c 49 mmol/mol; UEC otherwise normal.

lead_in: Which factor is most likely to have precipitated this hypoglycaemic episode?

options.text: The basal insulin glargine dose, which should be reduced as the default first action
options.rationale: Insulin glargine alone is unlikely to cause this severity in a previously stable patient; reflexive dose reduction without identifying precipitants misses the actual cause. The anti-pattern is treating insulin as default culprit when sulfonylurea-antibiotic interaction is present. Glargine dose review is appropriate after identifying and addressing the precipitant (RACGP 2024; AMH).

options.text: Trimethoprim potentiating gliclazide via reduced clearance in CKD plus the reduced oral intake
options.rationale: Trimethoprim potentiates sulfonylureas (CYP2C9 inhibition and competitive renal tubular secretion) and gliclazide accumulates with reduced eGFR; together with poor intake and weight loss, this is the most likely precipitant. Sulfonylureas should be reviewed in CKD and with new interacting antibiotics (AMH; eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Direct metformin-induced hypoglycaemia from her usual twice-daily 1 g metformin dose
options.rationale: Metformin does not cause hypoglycaemia in monotherapy and only minimally contributes in combination therapy because it does not stimulate insulin secretion. The anti-pattern is blaming the most familiar drug. Metformin contributes to hypoglycaemia only in extreme overdose or via lactic acidosis indirectly (AMH).

options.text: Cefalexin-induced hypoglycaemia from the recently started urinary tract infection antibiotic
options.rationale: Cefalexin is not a recognised cause of hypoglycaemia. Among antibiotics, fluoroquinolones (gatifloxacin most strongly) and trimethoprim are the main offenders potentiating sulfonylureas. Cefalexin causes hypoglycaemia only through reduced oral intake from nausea, not pharmacologically (AMH).

options.text: Inadequate paramedic glucagon dose given prior to her arrival in the emergency department
options.rationale: 1 mg IM glucagon is the standard adult dose and her glucose responded - this is not the cause of the original event. The anti-pattern is blaming the rescue rather than the precipitant. Glucagon dose review is appropriate only when initial response fails or in glycogen-depleted states (alcohol, prolonged fasting) (ANZCOR; AMH).

explanation.summary: Severe hypoglycaemia in an older patient on a sulfonylurea plus basal insulin should prompt review of recently introduced interacting drugs (trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole) and declining renal function. Sulfonylureas are best avoided in significant CKD and should usually be ceased before reflexively reducing insulin (RACGP 2024; AMH).
key_points: Trimethoprim, fluoroquinolones, fluconazole potentiate sulfonylureas
Sulfonylureas accumulate in CKD - avoid below eGFR ~30 to 45 mL/min/1.73m^2
Always look for missed meals, weight loss, new infections, new drugs
Older adults with HbA1c <53 mmol/mol on sulfonylurea + insulin are over-treated
explanation.pearls: Hypoglycaemia unawareness develops with recurrent lows - target a slightly higher HbA1c in frail older adults (53 to 64 mmol/mol).

stem: A 38-year-old woman is brought to ED with two days of worsening agitation, vomiting and fever after stopping carbimazole 10 days ago because she ran out of her script. She was diagnosed with Graves disease six months ago. On arrival she is agitated, sweaty and tremulous. She is in atrial fibrillation at 152 bpm with a blood pressure of 96/52 mmHg. Temperature is 39.6 degrees and she has had one episode of vomiting in ED. She has bilateral exophthalmos and a smooth diffuse goitre. Her last TSH four weeks ago was <0.01 mIU/L with free T4 48 pmol/L. She is alert but confused (GCS 14) and not following commands consistently. There is no chest pain. Bedside echo at the bedside shows preserved LV function. Capillary glucose is 7.2 mmol/L. She has no recent travel, no respiratory symptoms and clear chest on auscultation. Beta-hCG is negative. CRP is 18 mg/L and white cell count is 11.2 x10^9/L without left shift. There is no rash and no neck stiffness. The endocrinology registrar has been called. She has no documented allergy to iodine, propranolol, propylthiouracil, carbimazole or hydrocortisone.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/52 mmHg, HR 152 (AF), RR 28, T 39.6 degC, SpO2 97 percent, GCS 14.
data_table.Examination: Agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic. Lid lag, exophthalmos, smooth goitre with bruit. Warm peripheries.
data_table.Investigations: TSH <0.01, fT4 48 (4 weeks ago); ECG AF rate 152; CXR clear; CRP 18; WCC 11.2; LFTs mildly deranged ALT 78.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial medical management?

options.text: IV propranolol, oral propylthiouracil, Lugol's iodine after PTU, hydrocortisone, paracetamol and active cooling
options.rationale: Thyroid storm (Burch-Wartofsky >=45) requires beta blockade, an antithyroid drug (PTU preferred for additional T4-to-T3 conversion blockade), iodine started AFTER the antithyroid drug to prevent substrate loading, hydrocortisone (blocks conversion and treats relative adrenal insufficiency) and supportive care including cooling and treatment of the precipitant (eTG Endocrinology; AMH).

options.text: IV amiodarone 300 mg for prompt rate control of her new atrial fibrillation
options.rationale: Amiodarone is 37% iodine by weight and PROVIDES the iodine substrate that worsens thyroid storm via the Jod-Basedow effect; it is contraindicated in thyrotoxicosis. Beta blockade (propranolol, esmolol) is preferred for rate control in storm. Amiodarone is a classic 'wrong drug at the wrong time' anti-pattern in thyroid emergency (AMH).

options.text: Empirical IV ceftriaxone 2 g and acyclovir for presumed sepsis or encephalitis
options.rationale: The clinical picture fits thyroid storm with a clear precipitant (medication non-adherence). Empirical antimicrobials may be reasonable if infection is also suspected but they do not address the thyroid emergency. Antibiotics first would be correct only if sepsis were the dominant working diagnosis with a separately identified source (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV levothyroxine 200 microgram and IV hydrocortisone 100 mg for presumed myxoedema coma
options.rationale: Myxoedema coma presents with hypothermia, bradycardia, hypoventilation, and depressed consciousness - the OPPOSITE picture (hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, tremor here). The anti-pattern is reflexively reaching for thyroid hormone in any thyroid emergency. T4 + steroid is correct only in confirmed myxoedema coma (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Oral carbimazole 60 mg single dose only and immediate discharge home with outpatient endocrinology follow-up
options.rationale: Thyroid storm carries 10 to 30% mortality without aggressive inpatient management; discharge is unsafe regardless of antithyroid drug. The anti-pattern is matching dose to thyrotoxicosis (which would be appropriate for ambulatory severe Graves) rather than to the storm phenotype. Outpatient carbimazole is correct for stable Graves disease, never for storm (eTG Endocrinology).

explanation.summary: Thyroid storm is a clinical diagnosis (Burch-Wartofsky score) with hyperthermia, tachyarrhythmia, CNS dysfunction, GI symptoms and a precipitant on a background of hyperthyroidism. Management combines beta blockade, antithyroid drug (PTU or carbimazole), iodine (after antithyroid drug), corticosteroid and treatment of the precipitant. Myxoedema coma is the opposite picture and requires IV thyroid hormone and hydrocortisone (eTG; AMH).
key_points: PTU preferred in storm (blocks peripheral T4->T3 conversion)
Iodine (Lugol's or potassium iodide) given >=1 hour AFTER antithyroid drug
Hydrocortisone covers relative adrenal insufficiency and blocks T4->T3
Avoid amiodarone; avoid aspirin (displaces T4 from binding proteins)
explanation.pearls: Myxoedema coma classically: hypothermia, bradycardia, hyponatraemia, hypoglycaemia, hypoventilation, depressed GCS - mirror image of storm.

stem: A 34-year-old woman is referred by her GP for definitive management of Graves disease diagnosed 14 months ago. She was treated with carbimazole 20 mg daily for 12 months, achieving biochemical euthyroidism, but TSH-receptor antibody titres remain markedly elevated (TRAb 8 IU/L) and she relapsed within six weeks of stopping carbimazole. She has moderate active Graves orbitopathy with proptosis, mild diplopia on upgaze and intermittent retro-orbital pain; CAS (Clinical Activity Score) is 4/7. She has not yet completed her family and would like to conceive within the next 12 months. She is a non-smoker now (ex-smoker, ceased six months ago). Her thyroid is moderately enlarged without nodularity, and ultrasound shows no discrete nodules. She has no compressive symptoms.

data_table.Examination: Diffuse smooth goitre, no nodules, no compressive symptoms. Proptosis with diplopia on upgaze, CAS 4/7.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.2 mIU/L, fT4 14 pmol/L on carbimazole 20 mg; TRAb 8 IU/L (relapsed off treatment); thyroid US no nodules.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate definitive treatment option?

options.text: Radioactive iodine 131I ablation arranged within the next month as definitive therapy
options.rationale: RAI can WORSEN active Graves orbitopathy (especially moderate-severe disease, smoking history, high TRAb), and pregnancy must be avoided for 6 months post-RAI - conflicting with her conception plans. RAI would be correct in Graves disease without orbitopathy, not pursuing pregnancy within 6 months, and no large goitre (ESA/ETA 2021).

options.text: Total thyroidectomy after pre-operative carbimazole and Lugol's iodine plus calcium and vitamin D loading
options.rationale: In Graves disease with moderate-to-severe active orbitopathy, large goitre, plans for pregnancy within 12 months, or patient preference, total thyroidectomy is preferred. It removes the antigenic stimulus, does not worsen orbitopathy and allows immediate trial of conception once levothyroxine is stable. Pre-operative preparation and a high-volume surgeon reduce complications (ETA 2021; eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: Indefinite low-dose carbimazole 5 mg once daily as long-term outpatient monotherapy
options.rationale: Long-term low-dose carbimazole is an emerging option for selected patients (with mild disease, low TRAb, no relapse history) but is less effective at definitive cure; high TRAb plus relapse history plus active orbitopathy favour definitive treatment, and carbimazole is teratogenic in early pregnancy. Long-term carbimazole is correct only in mild persistent disease where definitive therapy is declined (eTG; AMH).

options.text: Switch to propylthiouracil 100 mg three times daily continued indefinitely for ongoing control
options.rationale: PTU carries higher risk of severe hepatotoxicity than carbimazole and is reserved for first trimester pregnancy (when carbimazole has higher teratogenicity) or thyroid storm. Indefinite PTU is the wrong long-term agent. PTU is correct in first trimester or storm only (AMH).

options.text: High-dose IV methylprednisolone 500 mg weekly for 6 weeks given alone as definitive treatment
options.rationale: IV methylprednisolone treats active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy but does NOT treat the underlying hyperthyroidism; it complements but does not replace definitive thyroid management. Methylprednisolone is correct as adjunctive therapy for orbitopathy (alongside definitive thyroid treatment), not as monotherapy (ETA 2021).

explanation.summary: Choice between carbimazole, radioactive iodine and thyroidectomy in Graves disease is guided by relapse risk (TRAb titre, goitre size, age, gender), pregnancy plans and orbitopathy status. Active moderate-to-severe orbitopathy is a relative contraindication to RAI; pregnancy within 6 months precludes RAI; large goitre or compressive symptoms favour surgery (ETA 2021; eTG).
key_points: Carbimazole 12 to 18 months: ~50 percent relapse after stopping
RAI: definitive but worsens orbitopathy in ~15 percent unless steroid prophylaxis given; defer pregnancy 6 months
Surgery: rapid cure, preserves option of pregnancy soon, removes antigen in severe orbitopathy
PTU reserved for first trimester pregnancy or thyroid storm
explanation.pearls: Smoking strongly worsens Graves orbitopathy and RAI outcomes - cessation is mandatory.

stem: A 32-year-old woman attends her GP for preconception counselling. She is planning pregnancy in the next 3 months. Blood tests ordered 8 weeks ago showed TSH 5.6 mIU/L with normal free T4. A repeat panel today shows TSH 5.4 mIU/L, free T4 13 pmol/L (normal), and TPO antibodies markedly elevated at 540 IU/mL. She has no goitre, no compressive symptoms, and no other thyroid history. She has hypercholesterolaemia on atorvastatin and is otherwise well. She has no constipation or weight change.

data_table.Examination: No goitre. No dry skin. Reflexes normal.
data_table.Investigations: TSH 6.8 mIU/L (was 7.2); fT4 14 pmol/L; TPO Ab 540 IU/mL (positive); lipids stable.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence levothyroxine to target a pre-conception TSH below 2.5 mIU/L and review TFTs in 6 to 8 weeks
options.rationale: In women planning pregnancy or in early pregnancy, Australian and international endocrine guidance recommends treating subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH above 2.5 in pregnancy planning, above trimester-specific limits in pregnancy) particularly when TPO antibodies are positive, to reduce miscarriage and pregnancy complications; pre-conception TSH target is below 2.5 mIU/L (ESA position; RACGP Red Book; ATA pregnancy guidelines).

options.text: Repeat TFTs and TPO antibodies in 6 to 12 months with no treatment now and advise contraception until reassessed
options.rationale: Deferring treatment delays pre-conception optimisation; positive TPO antibodies plus TSH above 2.5 in a woman planning pregnancy is a treatment indication (ESA; ATA).

options.text: Stop atorvastatin and recheck thyroid function in 6 weeks since statin may interfere with the TSH assay
options.rationale: Statins do not interfere with TSH; atorvastatin should be ceased on confirmation of pregnancy due to fetal risk, but not for thyroid reasons (AMH).

options.text: Refer for ultrasound-guided fine needle aspiration of the thyroid for cytology
options.rationale: FNA is indicated for suspicious thyroid nodules; she has no nodule (RACGP).

options.text: Commence T3 (liothyronine) 20 microgram daily as primary thyroid replacement
options.rationale: T3 monotherapy is not recommended for primary hypothyroidism; levothyroxine T4 is the standard (AMH; ESA).

explanation.summary: Subclinical hypothyroidism is defined by an elevated TSH with a normal free T4. Treatment is generally reserved for TSH persistently >=10 mIU/L, or 4 to 10 with attributable symptoms, pregnancy, infertility or large goitre. Otherwise, monitoring is appropriate; positive TPO antibodies confirm Hashimoto and predict progression (ESA; RACGP).
key_points: Confirm with a repeat after 6 to 12 weeks before labelling
Treat if TSH >=10, symptomatic with TSH 4 to 10, pregnant or planning pregnancy
Start levothyroxine 1.6 microgram/kg/day (lower in elderly and IHD)
Recheck TFTs 6 to 8 weeks after dose changes
explanation.pearls: In pregnancy, treat subclinical hypothyroidism if TSH is above the trimester-specific upper limit (typically >4.0 mIU/L).

stem: A 52-year-old woman is reviewed on the surgical ward 14 hours after total thyroidectomy for a multinodular goitre with compressive symptoms. She reports peri-oral and finger tingling for the last hour and one episode of carpal spasm when the cuff was inflated. She is otherwise well, with no airway compromise and no haematoma at the wound. She is on regular paracetamol and prophylactic enoxaparin. Bloods drawn at the onset of symptoms show: corrected calcium 1.78 mmol/L (corrected for albumin 38 g/L), ionised calcium 0.92 mmol/L, magnesium 0.74 mmol/L, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, parathyroid hormone 0.4 pmol/L (low), 25-OH vitamin D 64 nmol/L, potassium 4.1, creatinine 76. Her ECG shows a prolonged QTc of 510 ms. She has no history of renal stones, no malignancy and her pre-operative calcium was 2.34 mmol/L. She is alert with GCS 15. There is no respiratory distress.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/78 mmHg, HR 84, RR 16, SpO2 98 percent on RA, T 36.7.
data_table.Examination: Peri-oral and finger paraesthesiae. Positive Chvostek and Trousseau signs. No stridor. Wound dry.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 1.78 mmol/L, ionised Ca 0.92; Mg 0.74; PTH 0.4 pmol/L; ECG QTc 510 ms.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Oral calcium carbonate 1.5 g elemental calcium with calcitriol 0.5 microgram and clinical review in 4 hours
options.rationale: Oral therapy is correct for asymptomatic mild hypocalcaemia or as maintenance after IV correction. Symptomatic acute hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV calcium for prompt myocardial protection. Oral calcium + calcitriol is the maintenance pathway after IV stabilisation (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL bolus followed by infusion, replace magnesium, then oral calcium plus calcitriol
options.rationale: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia with tetany or QT prolongation requires IV 10 percent calcium gluconate bolus followed by infusion to prevent rebound. Magnesium replacement is required if low because it perpetuates hypocalcaemia. Oral calcium and active vitamin D (calcitriol) are added for maintenance, with active D needed because of low PTH (eTG Endocrinology; AMH). Practically: 10 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate (2.2 mmol Ca) in 100 mL of 5 percent dextrose over 10 minutes, then infusion such as 100 mL of 10 percent calcium gluconate in 1 L over 6 to 8 hours, with active vitamin D needed because PTH is low.

options.text: IV 10 percent calcium chloride 10 mL given via a peripheral cannula stat dose
options.rationale: Calcium chloride contains 3x more elemental calcium per mL but is highly irritant and risks severe extravasation injury via peripheral line; calcium gluconate is preferred for peripheral administration. Calcium chloride is correct only via central venous access or in cardiac arrest (AMH; eTG).

options.text: IV magnesium sulfate 10 mmol over 20 minutes given as the only acute therapy
options.rationale: Magnesium 0.74 mmol/L is borderline and replacement is appropriate (low Mg perpetuates hypocalcaemia by impairing PTH release and action), but Mg alone will not correct her acute symptomatic hypocalcaemia in the required timeframe. Mg-alone is correct in pure hypomagnesaemic hypocalcaemia with mild symptoms (eTG Endocrinology).

options.text: IV cinacalcet 30 mg given as a single calcimimetic dose for acute symptom control
options.rationale: Cinacalcet is a calcimimetic that LOWERS calcium in hyperparathyroidism; it is contraindicated in hypocalcaemia and would worsen her presentation - a classic wrong-direction-drug anti-pattern. Cinacalcet is correct in primary or secondary hyperparathyroidism, never in hypocalcaemia (AMH).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic hypocalcaemia (tetany, seizures, laryngospasm, QT prolongation, prolonged ECG changes) is treated with IV 10 percent calcium gluconate bolus and infusion, replacement of magnesium if low, and transition to oral calcium plus active vitamin D (calcitriol or alfacalcidol) once stable. Calcitriol is required in hypoparathyroidism because PTH-dependent 1-alpha-hydroxylation is impaired (eTG; AMH).
key_points: 10 percent calcium gluconate 10 mL = 2.2 mmol elemental Ca (safer peripherally)
Always replete magnesium - low Mg causes refractory hypocalcaemia
Active vitamin D (calcitriol) needed when PTH is low
Continuous ECG monitoring during IV infusion
explanation.pearls: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcaemia: check Ca at 6, 12 and 24 hours; transient post-op hypoparathyroidism is common, permanent in 1 to 3 percent.

stem: A 52-year-old man is on day 7 of his admission to neurosurgery after a Hunt-Hess 3 aneurysmal subarachnoid haemorrhage clipped on day 1. On the morning ward round his nurse reports increased confusion and lethargy. His urine output over the last 24 hours has been 4.2 L. He has been on free water orally without IV fluids since day 4. Bloods today: sodium 122 mmol/L (was 134 on day 4), potassium 3.6, urea 9.4 mmol/L, creatinine 96 micromol/L, serum osmolality 258 mosm/kg, glucose 5.4 mmol/L. Urine: sodium 95 mmol/L, osmolality 540 mosm/kg, specific gravity 1.022. Clinically he has dry mucous membranes, BP 102/64 mmHg (was 128/74 yesterday) lying with a 14 mmHg systolic drop on standing, HR 96, normal JVP and no oedema. Bedside lung ultrasound shows A-lines bilaterally. Weight is down 2 kg from admission.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64 lying with 14 mmHg systolic postural drop, HR 96, T 36.7, SpO2 98 percent.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, JVP not visible, no oedema, weight down 2 kg.
data_table.Investigations: Na 122 mmol/L (was 134), K 3.6, urea 9.4, Cr 96, serum osm 258; urine Na 95, urine osm 540; UO 4.2 L/24h.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial fluid management?

options.text: Fluid restriction to 800 mL per day with daily sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Fluid restriction is correct for SIADH (euvolaemic hyponatraemia); this patient is hypovolaemic with polyuria, weight loss, and postural hypotension after SAH. Restricting fluid would worsen cerebral hypoperfusion and the risk of delayed cerebral ischaemia (DCI). The anti-pattern is treating any post-neurosurgical hyponatraemia as SIADH without volume assessment (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 125 mL/hour with oral salt, escalating to 3% saline if sodium fails to rise
options.rationale: The clinical picture (polyuria, weight loss, postural hypotension, dry mucous membranes after SAH) plus high urine sodium fits cerebral salt wasting (CSW), where treatment is volume and sodium replacement, not restriction. Maintaining euvolaemia and avoiding hyponatraemia reduces delayed cerebral ischaemia in SAH (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally given today with hourly sodium monitoring on the ward
options.rationale: Tolvaptan (V2 antagonist) treats SIADH by increasing free water clearance; it would worsen hypovolaemic hyponatraemia in CSW and is contraindicated in volume depletion. The anti-pattern is using SIADH-specific therapy without confirming the volume state. Tolvaptan is correct only in confirmed euvolaemic SIADH (AMH).

options.text: IV 3 percent hypertonic saline 150 mL bolus over 10 minutes only, with no further fluid
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline bolus is correct for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizures, coma) for a target rise of 4 to 6 mmol/L in 1 to 2 hours, but a single bolus without ongoing maintenance volume in CSW under-treats volume depletion and risks overshoot. Hypertonic saline is correct as part of CSW management when sodium fails to rise on isotonic saline, not as sole therapy (ENLS 2023).

options.text: IV 5 percent dextrose at 100 mL/hour to reduce osmotic stress on the brain
options.rationale: Free water in 5% dextrose worsens hyponatraemia and is contraindicated; in any neurocritical patient it also increases intracranial water content. This is a major anti-pattern in neurocritical care. Dextrose is appropriate only for documented hypoglycaemia (ENLS 2023).

explanation.summary: After SAH or other brain injury, distinguishing SIADH from CSW depends on volume status. Both have low serum osmolality and inappropriately concentrated urine with high urine sodium. CSW shows hypovolaemia (weight loss, postural hypotension, dry mucous membranes, polyuria); SIADH shows euvolaemia. CSW is treated with isotonic saline plus sodium replacement; SIADH with fluid restriction (or tolvaptan if persistent). Limit sodium correction to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination (ENLS 2023; Australian Stroke Foundation).
key_points: Volume status is THE distinguishing feature between SIADH and CSW
After SAH, hypotonic fluids are avoided; isotonic or hypertonic preferred
Symptomatic severe hyponatraemia: 3 percent saline 150 mL bolus, target rise 4 to 6 mmol/L
Limit correction to 8 to 10 mmol/L per 24 h (6 to 8 if high risk for ODS)
explanation.pearls: If sodium overcorrects, re-lower with DDAVP and 5 percent dextrose - cerebral pontine myelinolysis is devastating and preventable.

subtopic_detail: SIADH vs cerebral salt wasting after SAH

stem: A 68-year-old right-handed man is brought to a metropolitan ED by ambulance. He was last seen well at 0815 by his wife; she returned at 1005 to find him slumped on the kitchen floor with right-sided weakness and aphasia. Time of arrival is 1110 (2 hours 55 minutes after last seen well). He has hypertension, ischaemic heart disease, atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VA 4) on apixaban 5 mg BD with his last dose at 0700. He has no history of recent surgery, head injury, bleeding or stroke. On arrival his blood pressure is 198/112 mmHg, glucose 6.8 mmol/L, GCS 12 (E3 V3 M6) and NIHSS is 18 (gaze preference, right hemiplegia, global aphasia, left neglect). Non-contrast CT brain shows no haemorrhage and an ASPECTS of 8 in the left MCA territory. CT angiography demonstrates a proximal left M1 occlusion with reasonable collaterals. There is a comprehensive stroke centre with thrombectomy capability 15 minutes away by road.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 198/112, HR 92 (AF), T 36.8, SpO2 97 percent on RA, GCS 12.
data_table.Examination: Right hemiplegia, global aphasia, left gaze preference. NIHSS 18.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 6.8 mmol/L; INR not on warfarin; apixaban 5 mg BD with last dose 0700; CT brain no haemorrhage, ASPECTS 8; CTA proximal M1 occlusion.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate next step in acute reperfusion management?

options.text: Administer IV alteplase immediately and then transfer for mechanical thrombectomy at the comprehensive stroke centre
options.rationale: IV thrombolysis is contraindicated within 48 hours of a DOAC dose unless DOAC levels are confirmed sub-therapeutic (anti-Xa for apixaban). His last apixaban was at 0700, well within 48 hours. The anti-pattern is reflex thrombolysis in any LVO without checking anticoagulant timing. Thrombolysis is correct only when DOAC is excluded or levels are sub-therapeutic (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Lower BP to under 140/90 mmHg with IV labetalol before making any reperfusion decision
options.rationale: Pre-thrombolysis BP target is <185/110 mmHg; for thrombectomy without thrombolysis higher BP is tolerated (typically <220/120). Aggressive lowering to <140/90 risks penumbral hypoperfusion and infarct extension. Tight BP control is correct only POST-thrombectomy with successful recanalisation (Australian Stroke Foundation; ENLS).

options.text: Activate stroke team and arrange urgent transfer for thrombectomy without thrombolysis, BP <185/110
options.rationale: Within 6 hours of last seen well, with a proximal M1 occlusion, NIHSS >=6 and ASPECTS >=6, mechanical thrombectomy is indicated. IV thrombolysis is contraindicated due to recent apixaban; proceed directly to thrombectomy. BP target pre-procedure is <185/110 (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).

options.text: Administer aspirin 300 mg orally and admit to the stroke unit for conservative management
options.rationale: Antiplatelet alone is not the reperfusion treatment for a proximal LVO; thrombectomy is the standard of care. Antiplatelet within 24 hours of thrombolysis would also be contraindicated. Aspirin-only is correct only for ischaemic stroke without LVO and outside reperfusion windows (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Administer IV idarucizumab to reverse apixaban first, then proceed to IV thrombolysis with alteplase
options.rationale: Idarucizumab reverses DABIGATRAN, not apixaban. Andexanet alfa partially reverses factor Xa inhibitors but is expensive, not universally available, and not routinely used to enable thrombolysis (risk of rebound). Direct transfer to thrombectomy avoids the reversal pitfall. The anti-pattern is conflating the two DOAC reversal agents (AMH; Australian Stroke Foundation).

explanation.summary: Acute ischaemic stroke reperfusion choices: IV thrombolysis within 4.5 hours of last seen well if no contraindication (BP <185/110, no recent DOAC dose within 48 h unless levels confirmed sub-therapeutic, no recent major surgery/bleeding/stroke). Mechanical thrombectomy within 6 hours for anterior circulation LVO (M1/ICA) with ASPECTS >=6 and disabling deficit; window extends to 24 hours if perfusion imaging shows salvageable penumbra. Thrombectomy is delivered with or without prior thrombolysis (Australian Stroke Foundation).
key_points: DOAC within 48 hours is a relative contraindication to thrombolysis unless levels measured
Thrombectomy window 0 to 6 h with CT/CTA; 6 to 24 h with perfusion imaging (DEFUSE-3, DAWN criteria)
Pre-thrombolysis BP target <185/110 mmHg
DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days then aspirin alone after minor stroke / high-risk TIA (CHANCE / POINT)
explanation.pearls: Door-to-needle target 60 minutes; door-to-groin target 90 minutes - process matters more than algorithm minutiae.

stem: A 72-year-old woman attends her GP three hours after an episode of left arm and face weakness lasting 35 minutes, now completely resolved. She has hypertension on amlodipine, T2DM on metformin, current smoker (20 pack-years), and no prior atrial fibrillation. Examination is now normal. BP today 168/96, blood glucose 9.2, ECG sinus rhythm, no carotid bruit. She has no anticoagulation, no bleeding tendency, and no recent head injury.

data_table.Examination: Normal neurological examination now. No carotid bruit. ECG sinus rhythm.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate combination of management steps?

options.text: Same-day ED transfer for non-contrast CT brain and CT angiography of head and neck, load with aspirin 300 mg now, and arrange admission to a stroke unit with cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines mandate urgent same-day assessment for all suspected TIAs regardless of ABCD2 score, with same-day brain and vessel imaging to identify large-vessel stenosis that may warrant urgent intervention, initial antiplatelet load, and cardiac monitoring to detect occult AF. DAPT for 21 days (aspirin plus clopidogrel) is then recommended after high-risk TIA per CHANCE/POINT data, but the initial step is urgent referral with aspirin loading.

options.text: Calculate ABCD2 and refer urgently only if 4 or more; otherwise manage in primary care with aspirin and review in 1 week
options.rationale: Australian guidance has moved away from ABCD2-based triage because lower scores still carry meaningful early stroke risk; all suspected TIAs require same-day specialist assessment (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Reassure and arrange outpatient specialist clinic review in 2 weeks with aspirin 100 mg daily today
options.rationale: Two-week deferral risks recurrent stroke; same-day specialist assessment with same-day imaging is the standard (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Commence rivaroxaban 20 mg daily empirically for presumed paroxysmal AF and arrange outpatient Holter monitor
options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation without documented AF exposes her to bleeding risk in the immediate post-TIA window before imaging excludes haemorrhage; rhythm monitoring is appropriate but not in place of urgent imaging (Australian Stroke Foundation).

options.text: Arrange a carotid Doppler within the next 4 weeks and review her again after the result is back
options.rationale: Carotid imaging is critical but must occur within 24 to 48 hours, not weeks; same-day referral with CTA is the standard pathway (Australian Stroke Foundation).

explanation.summary: Modern Australian guidance for TIA emphasises urgent same-day specialist assessment regardless of ABCD2 score. Brain and vascular imaging, antiplatelet initiation (DAPT for 21 days after high-risk TIA or minor non-cardioembolic stroke per CHANCE/POINT), risk factor control and rhythm monitoring for occult AF underpin secondary prevention (Australian Stroke Foundation Living Guidelines).
key_points: All suspected TIAs: same-day specialist assessment
Aspirin 300 mg loading, then DAPT 21 days for high-risk TIA / minor stroke
BP, lipid, glucose, smoking cessation, anticoagulation if AF detected
Carotid imaging within 24 to 48 hours for anterior circulation events
explanation.pearls: ABCD2 is still useful as a teaching tool but does not safely triage TIA to deferred care.

stem: A 34-year-old man is brought to ED by his partner after a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 90 seconds while watching television. He has no prior seizures, no head injuryo medications. He was confused for about 20 minutes after the event and bit the side of his tongue. On arrival he is back to his baseline. Capillary glucose, electrolytes, calcium, magnesium and a urine drug screen are normal. ECG is normal sinus rhythm. He works as a self-employed delivery driver using a standard private licence (no commercial endorsement). His non-contrast CT brain is normal. He wants to know when he can drive and what investigations are needed. He has no fever, no headacherology. He has no family history of epilepsy and no recent travel. He is otherwise well and has not started any new medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 76, T 36.8, SpO2 99 percent, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Normal neurology. Lateral tongue bite. No focal weakness.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose normal; electrolytes, Ca, Mg, drug screen all normal; CT brain normal; ECG normal.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate combination of next investigation and driving advice?

options.text: MRI brain and EEG as outpatient; no driving for at least 6 months (Austroads private standard)
options.rationale: First unprovoked seizure workup includes outpatient MRI brain (preferred over CT for structural lesions) and EEG to assess recurrence risk. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 requires a minimum 6-month non-driving period after a first seizure for private licence, longer for commercial. The treating doctor must counsel and document this (Austroads AFTD 2022; eTG Neurology).

options.text: Commence sodium valproate immediately and clear him to return to driving in 1 month
options.rationale: Antiepileptic monotherapy after a single unprovoked seizure with no abnormalities is not routinely recommended; the modest reduction in recurrence is offset by drug burden. 1-month suspension is well short of the Austroads minimum (6 months private). Valproate is teratogenic and inappropriate in a woman of reproductive age without explicit pre-conception counselling. Multiple errors (eTG Neurology; Austroads).

options.text: Lumbar puncture today and start IV ceftriaxone for presumed bacterial meningitis empirically
options.rationale: There are no features of meningitis (no fever, headache, neck stiffness, altered consciousness); LP is not indicated for an isolated first seizure. The anti-pattern is overinvestigating in low-pretest-probability scenarios. LP is correct with fever, headache, or focal neurology suggestive of CNS infection (eTG Antibiotic).

options.text: Continuous video-EEG admission for 72 hours starting today as the immediate next investigation
options.rationale: Continuous video-EEG is for diagnostic uncertainty (PNES vs epilepsy) or pre-surgical evaluation, not standard first-seizure workup. The anti-pattern is escalating to specialised testing before standard workup. Routine outpatient EEG (with sleep deprivation if first study negative) is the standard first investigation (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Cleared to return to commercial driving as soon as he feels well, neurology in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Commercial driving requires longer abstinence under Austroads AFTD: typically 10 years seizure-free off medication, or 5 years on medication with strict conditions. Immediate return is unsafe and legally non-compliant. The treating doctor must specifically document commercial-vs-private status (Austroads AFTD 2022).

explanation.summary: After a first unprovoked seizure in an adult, MRI brain and EEG outpatient form the standard recurrence-risk workup. Antiepileptic drug initiation is not routine but may be considered if recurrence risk is high (epileptiform EEG, structural lesion). Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 mandates a minimum 6-month non-driving period for private vehicles after a first seizure (longer with risk factors), and longer abstinence for commercial vehicles (eTG Neurology).
key_points: Workup: MRI brain, EEG, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, magnesium, drug screen
AED start considered if recurrence risk high (e.g. structural lesion, epileptiform EEG, nocturnal seizure)
Private vehicle: minimum 6 months off driving after first seizure
Commercial vehicle: minimum 5 years seizure-free off medication
explanation.pearls: Counselling and documentation of driving restriction is the doctor's legal and professional duty - patients self-report to road authority.

stem: A 56-year-old man with known epilepsy on sodium valproate is brought to ED in convulsive status epilepticus. The seizure started 25 minutes ago in the ambulance. Paramedics gave 10 mg IM midazolam at 12 minutes and another 10 mg IV at 20 minutes without resolution; he continues to have generalised tonic-clonic activity. On arrival his airway is maintained with nasopharyngeal airway and SpO2 96 percent on 15 L non-rebreather. BP is 156/92, HR 124, temperature 37.1. Capillary glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. He has IV access and is now 27 minutes into the seizure. He has no recent head injury and his valproate level is pending. There is no fever, no rash, and no obvious focal sepsis. His airway position is maintained and there is no audible stridor. His pupils are equal and reactive. Bedside ECG shows sinus tachycardia without ischaemic changes.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 156/92, HR 124, RR 18 (assisted), T 37.1, SpO2 96 percent on 15 L NRB, GCS 3 during seizure.
data_table.Examination: Generalised tonic-clonic seizure continuing. Airway maintained with NPA.
data_table.Investigations: Glucose 7.4 mmol/L; valproate level pending; ECG sinus tachycardia.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Repeat a further IV midazolam 10 mg bolus dose as third benzodiazepine attempt
options.rationale: Two adequate benzodiazepine doses have already been given with no response; further benzodiazepine risks respiratory depression without added benefit. The anti-pattern is repeating first-line when escalation is mandated. Escalate to second-line antiepileptic (levetiracetam, phenytoin, or valproate) (eTG Neurology; ANZCOR).

options.text: IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (maximum 4.5 g) given over 10 minutes as second-line agent
options.rationale: After two adequate doses of a benzodiazepine, second-line therapy in convulsive status epilepticus is IV levetiracetam, phenytoin (or fos-phenytoin), or sodium valproate. Levetiracetam at 60 mg/kg over 10 minutes is recommended by ESETT and current Australian eTG and NSW Health guidance (eTG Neurology; ESETT 2019).

options.text: IV propofol 2 mg/kg induction and intubate without any further antiepileptic drug given
options.rationale: Anaesthetic induction is third-line, after a second-line antiepileptic has failed; jumping to anaesthesia skips a step and commits to ICU intubation unnecessarily. Propofol induction is correct in confirmed refractory status epilepticus (failure of second-line agent) (eTG Neurology).

options.text: Oral phenytoin 300 mg administered via nasogastric tube as the next antiepileptic agent
options.rationale: Oral phenytoin via NG tube has unpredictable absorption and is too slow for acute status; IV phenytoin (20 mg/kg, max rate 50 mg/min) is the parenteral option if levetiracetam is not chosen. The anti-pattern is using oral route in an emergency. NG phenytoin is correct only for outpatient initiation or maintenance, not acute status (eTG Neurology; AMH).

options.text: IV thiamine 200 mg and 50 mL of 50 percent dextrose given as the next intervention
options.rationale: Empirical glucose is reserved for hypoglycaemia; his glucose is 7.4 mmol/L. Thiamine is appropriate if alcohol use disorder is suspected (always BEFORE glucose to prevent Wernicke precipitation) but does not terminate the seizure. Thiamine + glucose is correct in suspected alcohol-related seizure with hypoglycaemia (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Convulsive status epilepticus is defined as a seizure lasting 5 minutes or more, or recurrent seizures without recovery of consciousness between, and carries a 20-30% mortality. The Australian stepwise approach is first-line benzodiazepine (IM midazolam 10 mg, IV lorazepam 4 mg or IV diazepam 10 mg) repeatable once at 5-10 minutes; second-line IV antiepileptic (levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, sodium valproate 40 mg/kg, or phenytoin 20 mg/kg) - the ESETT trial showed these are roughly equivalent and levetiracetam is preferred for its tolerability; third-line refractory status requires intubation with propofol, midazolam or thiopental infusions and continuous EEG. Imaging, lumbar puncture and toxicology workup all happen in parallel with - never instead of - seizure termination. Common reversible drivers in adults include anticonvulsant non-adherence, alcohol withdrawal, hypoglycaemia, hyponatraemia and CNS infection.
key_points: Two benzodiazepine doses then escalate, do not repeat indefinitely
Second-line: levetiracetam, phenytoin or valproate (ESETT showed equivalent efficacy)
Refractory (>30 min despite second-line): intubation and continuous anaesthetic infusion
Always check glucose; consider thiamine, eclampsia, hyponatraemia, toxic causes
explanation.pearls: In benzodiazepine-refractory status, IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (max 4.5 g) over 10 minutes is non-inferior to phenytoin and sodium valproate (ESETT 2019) and is the default Australian second-line agent for its ease and safety profile.

stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to her GP with a three-week history of new bilateral temporal headache progressively worsening, with scalp tenderness on hair brushing and jaw pain with chewing tough food. Yesterday she had a 10-minute episode of curtain-like vision loss in her right eye that has now resolved. Today on examination her right eye visual acuity is 6/9 (was 6/6 four months ago at optometry) with a relative afferent pupillary defect; left eye 6/6. Temporal arteries tender and pulsatile bilaterally. Bloods: ESR 92 mm/hr, CRP 84 mg/L, Hb 108 g/L, platelets 484. She has hypertension on perindopril and T2DM on metformin (HbA1c 58 mmol/mol). Bone mineral density was T-score -1.4 at the lumbar spine 6 months ago.

data_table.Examination: Tender pulsatile bilateral temporal arteries. Normal fundoscopy. VA 6/9 R, 6/6 L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Admit for IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days followed by oral prednisolone 60 mg daily, with same-day ophthalmology and rheumatology review and arrangement of temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: Amaurosis fugax and a new relative afferent pupillary defect with reduced visual acuity indicate ophthalmic involvement of giant cell arteritis, which is a sight-threatening emergency requiring IV pulse methylprednisolone rather than oral prednisolone alone. Biopsy can still be performed within 1 to 2 weeks of steroid initiation. Calcium, vitamin D and PJP and bone protection planning are needed but not immediate (eTG Rheumatology; ARA position).

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 60 mg daily today and arrange temporal artery biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks
options.rationale: Oral prednisolone 60 mg is standard for GCA without visual involvement; the presence of amaurosis fugax and RAPD escalates to IV pulse methylprednisolone (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Order CT brain today and start prednisolone only if temporal artery biopsy is positive within 2 weeks
options.rationale: CT brain does not diagnose GCA and waiting for biopsy risks permanent blindness within hours to days; steroid must be started on clinical suspicion (eTG).

options.text: Commence tocilizumab subcutaneous 162 mg weekly as first-line monotherapy
options.rationale: Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing add-on therapy for established disease; it does not replace acute high-dose corticosteroid in the emergency setting (eTG; ARA).

options.text: Treat empirically as migraine with sumatriptan 50 mg and arrange neurology follow-up in 2 weeks
options.rationale: New severe headache in someone over 50 with raised inflammatory markers, jaw claudication and visual loss is not migraine, and triptans are contraindicated in suspected ischaemic disease (eTG Neurology).

explanation.summary: Giant cell arteritis is a medical emergency due to risk of permanent visual loss. Diagnosis is clinical (age >=50, new headache, jaw claudication, temporal artery tenderness, raised ESR/CRP) supported by temporal artery biopsy (skip lesions; sensitivity ~75 percent) or vascular ultrasound (halo sign). High-dose corticosteroid is started immediately, with biopsy within 1 to 2 weeks. Tocilizumab is steroid-sparing in established disease (eTG Rheumatology).
key_points: Visual symptoms or jaw claudication: IV methylprednisolone 0.5 to 1 g daily for 3 days then oral prednisolone
Otherwise: oral prednisolone 40 to 60 mg daily
Aspirin 100 mg daily reduces ischaemic complications (controversial; review individual bleeding risk)
Tocilizumab and methotrexate are steroid-sparing options
Bone protection (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate), PCP prophylaxis if high-dose long-duration steroid
explanation.pearls: ESR and CRP are usually both elevated; isolated normal CRP makes GCA very unlikely.

stem: A 28-year-old woman is brought to ED 25 minutes after eating a peanut-containing dessert. She has a known peanut allergy. On arrival: stridor and dyspnoea, widespread urticaria and lip swelling, BP 78/42, HR 132, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L non-rebreather, RR 28, alert but distressed. You give IM adrenaline 0.5 mg into the anterolateral mid-thigh and lie her supine with legs elevated. Five minutes later, BP is 82/46, HR 130, RR 28, SpO2 92 percent, with persistent stridor. A second IM adrenaline 0.5 mg is given. Five minutes after that, BP is 84/48, HR 128, SpO2 92 percent, stridor persists. Two large-bore IVs are in. She has no past cardiac history and takes no regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/42, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 91 percent on 15 L NRB, T 36.6, GCS 15.
data_table.Examination: Stridor, widespread urticaria, lip swelling, accessory muscle use.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV adrenaline infusion at 5 microgram per minute titrated to clinical response, give 20 mL/kg IV crystalloid bolus, and prepare for advanced airway management
options.rationale: After two doses of IM adrenaline without adequate response, ASCIA recommends IV adrenaline infusion (5 to 15 microgram per minute titrated) alongside aggressive fluid resuscitation; persistent stridor and shock require airway preparation including senior anaesthetic and ENT support, with surgical airway equipment ready (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).

options.text: Continue IM adrenaline 0.5 mg every 5 minutes indefinitely and avoid intravenous adrenaline
options.rationale: Refractory anaphylaxis (no response after two IM doses) is the indication for IV adrenaline infusion under monitored conditions; persisting with IM alone delays effective therapy (ASCIA 2024).

options.text: Give IV hydrocortisone 200 mg and IV promethazine 25 mg and observe for further response
options.rationale: Steroids and antihistamines are adjuncts only and do not reverse acute anaphylaxis; promethazine can worsen hypotension; neither addresses refractory disease (ASCIA 2024; AMH).

options.text: Give IV glucagon 1 to 5 mg bolus as the next escalation step
options.rationale: Glucagon is reserved for refractory anaphylaxis in patients on beta-blocker; she is on no medications, so IV adrenaline infusion is the next step (ASCIA 2024).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 5 mg and nebulised adrenaline 5 mg via oxygen-driven nebuliser, then reassess
options.rationale: Nebulised therapies are adjuncts for persistent bronchospasm or laryngeal oedema after systemic adrenaline; they do not replace IV adrenaline infusion in refractory shock (ASCIA 2024).

explanation.summary: IM adrenaline is the single most important treatment in anaphylaxis. Adult dose is 0.5 mg of 1:1000 (10 microgram/kg, maximum 0.5 mg) into the anterolateral mid-thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Place supine with legs elevated unless airway compromise. Give high-flow oxygen and IV fluid (20 mL/kg crystalloid). Corticosteroids and antihistamines are adjuncts only (ASCIA 2024; ANZCOR).
key_points: IM adrenaline first, IV adrenaline infusion if refractory after 2 to 3 doses
Supine with legs elevated, high-flow oxygen, IV crystalloid bolus
Observe for biphasic reaction for at least 4 hours (longer if severe)
Discharge with EpiPen (or two), ASCIA action plan, allergy referral
explanation.pearls: If the patient is on a beta blocker and refractory, glucagon 1 to 5 mg IV may help by bypassing the beta receptor.
